questions.question:
A binary search tree T contains n distinct elements. What is the time complexity of picking an element in T that is smaller than the maximum element in T?
A. Theta(log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(log n)
D. Theta(1)
questions.to_find: Time complexity of finding any element smaller than the maximum in a BST

understand.plain: We have a BST with n distinct elements and we want to find ANY element that is smaller than the maximum element. We do not need to find the second largest or anything specific — just any element smaller than the max. Since a BST has more than one element (n >= 2), the root itself (or any non-maximum node) qualifies. We can pick such an element in O(1) time.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every left subtree node < parent node < every right subtree node.
The rightmost node in the BST — found by always going right from the root.
keywords.example: In a BST with values 3,5,7, the maximum is 7 (rightmost node).

understand.visual_alt: A BST with root node; any node except the rightmost leaf is smaller than the maximum.

given.aim: Find the time complexity of retrieving any element smaller than the maximum in a BST of n distinct elements.

terms.meaning: Number of distinct elements in the BST
terms.example: n = 7 elements stored in the tree
terms.connects: The size of the BST; n >= 2 for the question to be meaningful

terms.meaning: The largest value in the BST, located at the rightmost node
terms.connects: We need an element strictly smaller than this

terms.meaning: We just need to find ONE element (not a specific one) that is < max
terms.example: The root, or left child, or any non-maximum node qualifies
terms.connects: The key insight: since we only need any such element, the root itself may work

given.plan: Since the BST has n distinct elements and n >= 2 (otherwise the question is trivial), the root itself is not always the maximum (the maximum is the rightmost node). We can check the root: if it is not the maximum, return it immediately. Even if the root happens to be the maximum (a degenerate right-skewed tree), we can return its left child or parent relationship — but more simply, we can return the root of the left subtree of the maximum, or simply note that we can always find and return such an element by accessing at most O(1) nodes (e.g., if root != max, return root; else root has no left child meaning tree has 1 element which contradicts n>=2 with distinct elements).

steps.title: Identify what is needed
steps.apply: We need to return ANY element from the BST that is strictly less than the maximum element. We do NOT need the second largest, nor any specific rank — just any element < max.
steps.note: This is a much weaker requirement than finding the second maximum.

steps.title: Observe structure of BST
steps.apply: In a BST with n >= 2 distinct elements, the maximum element is at the rightmost node. The root of the tree is accessible in O(1). If the root is not the maximum (i.e., the tree is not a right-degenerate single-path tree with root = max, which would require root to have no left subtree), then the root itself is a valid answer. Even in a right-skewed BST where root < max, the root qualifies. In any BST with n >= 2, we can always return the root if it is not the max, or the left child of the max if it exists — all O(1) accesses.
steps.note: The root is always accessible in O(1). In a BST the maximum is the rightmost node; the root is smaller than the max unless the tree has exactly 1 element.

steps.title: Conclude time complexity
steps.apply: Accessing the root (one pointer dereference) and returning it as the element smaller than max takes O(1) = Theta(1) time, regardless of n.
steps.note: This is a classic trick question: asking for 'any' element instead of a specific one drastically reduces complexity.

solution.result: The time complexity is Theta(1) — Option D.

questions.formula_note: Key insight: finding any element (not a specific one) smaller than the maximum in a BST requires only O(1) time since the root is always accessible and is almost always smaller than the maximum.

questions.question: Suppose a binary search tree with 15 distinct elements is also a complete binary tree. The tree is stored using the array representation of binary heap trees. Assuming that the array indices start with 1, the largest element of the tree is stored at index ______________ .
questions.to_find: Array index where the largest element is stored when a complete BST with 15 elements is stored using heap-style array representation

understand.plain: We have a complete binary tree with 15 nodes that is also a BST. When we store a complete binary tree in an array (like a heap), node at index i has children at 2i and 2i+1. In a BST, the largest element is the rightmost node. We need to find which array index corresponds to the rightmost node in a complete binary tree of 15 nodes.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where all levels are fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled left to right.
keywords.example: A tree with 7 nodes has 3 full levels; a tree with 8 nodes has the 8th node as leftmost on level 4.

keywords.explain: A way to store a complete binary tree in an array: root at index 1, left child of node i at 2i, right child at 2i+1.
keywords.example: Root at index 1, its left child at 2, right child at 3, left child of node 2 at 4, etc.

keywords.explain: In a BST, the largest element is always the rightmost node (keep going right from root until no right child).
keywords.example: BST with 1..7: root=4, right subtree has 5,6,7; rightmost is 7.

understand.visual_alt: Complete binary tree with 15 nodes stored as array indices 1 to 15. The rightmost leaf at index 15 (highlighted) is the largest element in the BST.

given.aim: Find the array index of the largest element in a complete BST with 15 nodes stored in heap-style array.

terms.meaning: Number of nodes in the complete binary tree / BST
terms.example: 15 nodes fill exactly 4 levels: level 1 has 1 node, level 2 has 2, level 3 has 4, level 4 has 8.
terms.connects: With 15 nodes = 2^4 - 1, the tree is a perfect binary tree — all 4 levels are completely full.

terms.meaning: Root at index 1; node at index i has children at 2i (left) and 2i+1 (right)
terms.example: Root=1, its children=2,3; node 2's children=4,5; node 3's children=6,7
terms.connects: Determines which index holds which structural position in the tree

terms.term: largest element in BST = rightmost node
terms.meaning: In a BST, following right children from root repeatedly leads to the maximum element
terms.example: From root (index 1), go right to index 3, then right to index 7, then right to index 15
terms.connects: Index 15 is the rightmost node in the complete tree of 15 nodes

given.plan: Since 15 = 2^4 - 1, the tree is a perfect binary tree with 4 levels. In the heap array, root is at index 1. The rightmost path follows right children: index 1 -> 3 -> 7 -> 15. So the largest element is at index 15.

steps.title: Identify the structure of the complete BST
steps.apply: With 15 nodes, this is a perfect binary tree (all levels completely filled). The height is 3 (0-indexed) or 4 levels total. Every internal node has exactly 2 children.
steps.note: 15 = 2^4 - 1 means it is a perfect binary tree, which is a special case of complete binary tree.

steps.title: Find the rightmost node using heap indexing
steps.apply:
In a BST, the maximum is the rightmost node. Starting from root at index 1, always follow the right child:
- Root: index 1
- Right child of 1: index 2*1+1 = 3
- Right child of 3: index 2*3+1 = 7
- Right child of 7: index 2*7+1 = 15
Node at index 15 has no children (15*2 = 30 > 15 and 15*2+1 = 31 > 15), so it is a leaf.
steps.note: The path 1 -> 3 -> 7 -> 15 represents the rightmost path in the perfect binary tree.

steps.title: Confirm index 15 is the rightmost leaf
steps.apply:
In the heap array representation of a 15-node perfect binary tree:
- Level 0 (root): index 1
- Level 1: indices 2-3
- Level 2: indices 4-7
- Level 3 (leaves): indices 8-15
The rightmost leaf is at index 15. Since this is a BST, the largest element is stored there.
steps.note: The last index (15) in the array corresponds to the rightmost position in the last level, which is the maximum in a BST.

solution.result: The largest element is stored at index 15.

questions.formula_note: Heap array representation: root at index 1, right child of node i at index 2i+1. BST maximum = rightmost node. For a perfect binary tree with 15 nodes, rightmost node is at index 15.

questions.question:
You are given a set S of distinct integers. A binary search tree T is created by inserting all elements of S one by one, starting with an empty tree. The tree T follows the convention that, at each node, all values stored in the left subtree of the node are smaller than the value stored at the node. You are not aware of the sequence in which these values were inserted into T, and you do not have access to S.

Which of the following statements is TRUE?
A. Inorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
B. Root of T can be determined from V.
C. Preorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
D. Postorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
questions.answer: A. Inorder traversal of T can be determined from V.
questions.to_find: Which traversal of the BST T can be determined from the values V (inorder traversal output) without knowing the insertion sequence

understand.plain: We are given the inorder traversal V of a BST T, but we do not know the insertion sequence or the original set directly. We need to determine which of the four traversals (inorder, root, preorder, postorder) can be uniquely reconstructed just from V. The inorder traversal of a BST always gives a sorted sequence. However, the shape of the BST depends on the insertion order, which is unknown — so the root, preorder, and postorder traversals all depend on the tree's shape. Wait — re-reading the question: we have V (the inorder traversal values, which is sorted), but we don't know the tree structure. So we cannot determine root, preorder, or postorder uniquely. But the inorder traversal is trivially V itself. Let me re-read carefully — actually, the question says we have access to V (the values from inorder traversal = sorted sequence) but not the insertion sequence. From V alone (sorted sequence), we cannot reconstruct the unique BST because different insertion orders give different BSTs with the same inorder traversal. However, we know the inorder traversal IS V (sorted). For preorder, postorder, and root, they depend on tree shape which is unknown.

keywords.explain: Left-Root-Right traversal; for a BST this always produces elements in sorted (ascending) order.
Root-Left-Right traversal; the first element is always the root. For a BST, this sequence uniquely determines the BST.

understand.visual_alt: Two different BSTs with same inorder traversal (same elements in sorted order) but different structures, showing why root/preorder/postorder differ.

given.aim: Determine which traversal output can be uniquely determined when given only the inorder traversal V of a BST T.

terms.meaning: The inorder traversal output of BST T (a sorted sequence of all elements in S)
terms.connects: V is given; it is sorted since T is a BST; from V alone we know the set S

terms.meaning: The order in which elements of S were inserted into the BST
terms.example: Inserting 3,1,4,2 vs inserting 2,1,3,4 gives different BST shapes
terms.connects: Unknown; different insertion sequences yield different tree shapes

terms.meaning: A BST is uniquely determined by its preorder traversal alone (or postorder alone), but NOT by inorder alone
terms.example: Given preorder [4,2,1,3,6,5,7] we can reconstruct the unique BST
terms.connects: Key fact to answer the question

given.plan: The inorder of a BST is always the sorted sequence — this is V itself, so inorder is trivially known (Option A is trivially true but perhaps the question has a different interpretation). Reading again: the question states we have V but don't know insertion sequence. The inorder traversal IS V by definition. For preorder/postorder/root, we need tree shape. Since we don't know insertion order, we cannot determine tree shape. So the ONLY traversal we can determine from V is the inorder traversal (= V itself, sorted). Answer is A.

steps.title: Understand what V represents
steps.apply: V is the inorder traversal of BST T. Since T is a BST and inorder traversal of any BST produces elements in sorted ascending order, V is simply the sorted sequence of all elements in S. From V we know the set S (all elements) but not the insertion order.
steps.note: V is fixed regardless of insertion order — it is always the sorted sequence of elements.

steps.title: Can we determine the inorder traversal from V?
steps.apply: Yes — the inorder traversal IS V by definition. No matter which BST T was constructed, its inorder traversal equals the sorted sequence V. So Option A is TRUE.
steps.note: The inorder traversal of any BST with a fixed set of elements is always the same sorted sequence.

steps.title: Can we determine root, preorder, or postorder from V?
steps.apply: The root, preorder, and postorder all depend on the shape of T, which depends on the insertion sequence. Since the insertion sequence is unknown, different BSTs can have the same inorder traversal (V) but different roots, preorder sequences, and postorder sequences. For example, inserting {1,2,3} as 2,1,3 gives a balanced BST; inserting as 1,2,3 gives a right-skewed BST. Both have inorder = [1,2,3] but different roots (2 vs 1) and different preorder/postorder sequences.
steps.note: Options B, C, D cannot be uniquely determined from V alone since the BST shape is ambiguous.

steps.title: Select the correct answer
steps.apply: From the analysis: Inorder traversal CAN be determined from V (it is V itself, sorted). Root, preorder, and postorder CANNOT be uniquely determined without knowing the insertion order. Therefore the correct answer is A.
steps.note: Looking at the options again from the image: the answer is A — inorder traversal of T can be determined from V.

solution.result: A. Inorder traversal of T can be determined from V. The inorder of any BST with the same set of elements is always the sorted sequence, which equals V.

questions.formula_note: Key property: inorder traversal of a BST always yields elements in sorted order, independent of insertion sequence. Preorder/postorder/root depend on tree shape (insertion order).

questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are TRUE for any binary search tree (BST) having n distinct integers?
A. The maximum length of a path from the root node to any other node is n - 1.
B. An inorder traversal will always produce a sorted sequence of elements.
C. Finding an element takes O(log n) time in the worst case.
D. Every BST is a Min-Heap.
questions.to_find: Which statements about BSTs with n distinct integers are always true

understand.plain: We need to evaluate four statements about BSTs and determine which are always true. A BST can be balanced or degenerate (skewed). We need to consider worst cases and general properties that hold for ALL BSTs, not just balanced ones.

keywords.explain: A BST where every node has at most one child, forming a linear chain (like a linked list).
keywords.example: Inserting 1,2,3,4,5 in order gives a right-skewed BST with height n-1.

keywords.explain: In a min-heap, every node's value is <= its children's values. This is NOT required for a BST.
keywords.example: BST with root=5, left=3, right=7: root=5 > left=3, so NOT a min-heap (min-heap needs root <= children).

understand.visual_alt: Comparison of a balanced BST (height log n) vs a skewed BST (height n-1), illustrating that worst-case path length is n-1.

given.aim: Identify all statements that are TRUE for any BST with n distinct integers.

terms.meaning: Number of edges from the root to a given node
terms.example: In a right-skewed BST of n nodes, the rightmost leaf is at depth n-1
terms.connects: Statement A claims maximum path length is n-1

terms.meaning: Left-Root-Right traversal of a binary tree
terms.connects: Statement B claims inorder always produces sorted output

terms.meaning: In the worst case (degenerate/skewed BST), search takes O(n) time, not O(log n)
terms.example: Searching in a right-skewed BST of n nodes requires up to n comparisons
terms.connects: Statement C is FALSE — O(log n) holds only for balanced BSTs

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A (max path = n-1: TRUE for degenerate BST), B (inorder = sorted: always TRUE by BST property), C (O(log n) search: FALSE in worst case for skewed BST, worst case is O(n)), D (every BST is min-heap: FALSE, BST order is different from heap order).

steps.title: Evaluate Statement A
steps.formula_raw: max path length = n - 1 (for degenerate BST)
steps.apply: A BST with n nodes can be completely skewed (e.g., inserting 1,2,3,...,n in order gives a right-skewed tree). In this case the path from root to the last node has n-1 edges. No BST can have a longer path than n-1 (since there are only n nodes). So the maximum possible path length from root to any node IS n-1. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: A path from root to a node of length n-1 means the tree has height n-1, which is the worst case for a degenerate BST.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement B
steps.formula_raw: BST inorder: Left < Root < Right => sorted output
steps.apply: By definition of a BST, for every node, all values in its left subtree are smaller and all values in its right subtree are larger. An inorder traversal (Left-Root-Right) always visits nodes in ascending order. This is a fundamental property of BSTs. Statement B is always TRUE.
steps.note: This holds for any BST regardless of its shape or how it was constructed.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement C
steps.apply: For a balanced BST, search is O(log n). However, for a degenerate (skewed) BST with n nodes, the height is n-1, and search in the worst case visits all n nodes. So the worst-case time for finding an element in ANY BST is O(n), not O(log n). Statement C is FALSE.
steps.note: O(log n) search applies only to balanced BSTs (like AVL or Red-Black trees), not to arbitrary BSTs.

steps.title: Evaluate Statement D
steps.apply: A min-heap requires: parent <= both children for every node. A BST requires: left subtree < root < right subtree. These are different properties. For example, a BST with root=5, left child=3, right child=7: the root (5) is greater than its left child (3), which violates the min-heap property (min-heap needs root <= left child). In general, a BST is NOT necessarily a min-heap. Statement D is FALSE.
steps.note: The BST ordering property (left < root < right) is fundamentally different from the heap ordering property (parent <= children).

solution.result: Statements A and B are TRUE. Answer: A, B.

questions.formula_note: Key BST properties: (1) Inorder traversal always produces sorted output. (2) Height of a BST can be as large as n-1 (degenerate case). (3) Worst-case search in a BST is O(n), not O(log n). (4) BST order != heap order.

questions.question: Suppose the values 10, -4, 5, 3, 20, 1, 15 are inserted in that order into an initially empty binary search tree. Let T be the resulting binary search tree. The number of edges in the path from the node containing 19 to the root node of T is _________. (Answer in integer)
questions.to_find: Number of edges from node containing 15 to root in the BST built by inserting 10, -4, 5, 3, 20, 1, 15

understand.plain: We insert seven values into an empty BST in a given order and then find how many edges are on the path from a specific node to the root. The number of edges in the path from a node to the root equals the depth of that node in the tree.

keywords.explain: To insert a value, compare with root: go left if smaller, go right if larger; repeat until finding an empty spot.
keywords.example: Insert 5 into BST with root 10: 5 < 10, go left; left is empty, so 5 becomes left child of 10.

keywords.explain: The number of edges from the root to that node. Root has depth 0.
keywords.example: Root's children have depth 1, grandchildren have depth 2, etc.

understand.visual_alt: BST built by inserting 10, -4, 5, 3, 20, 1, 15 in order. Root=10, left=-4, right=20; -4 has right child 5; 5 has left child 3; 3 has left child 1; 20 has left child 15. Path from 15 to root: 15->20->10 = 2 edges.

given.aim: Build the BST by inserting 10, -4, 5, 3, 20, 1, 15 in order, then find the number of edges from node 15 to the root.

terms.example: First element inserted becomes the root
terms.connects: Determines the exact structure of the BST

terms.meaning: The unique path going upward from a node to the root, counted in edges
terms.example: A node at depth 3 has 3 edges to the root
terms.connects: We need to count this for node 15

terms.meaning: New value < current node => go left; new value > current node => go right
terms.example: Inserting 15: start at root 10; 15>10 go right to 20; 15<20 go left; left of 20 is empty, so 15 goes there
terms.connects: Determines where 15 ends up in the tree

given.plan: Simulate the BST insertions step by step to build the tree, then trace the path from node 15 to the root and count edges.

steps.title: Build the BST step by step
steps.apply:
Insert 10: root = 10.
Insert -4: -4 < 10, go left; left of 10 is empty => -4 is left child of 10.
Insert 5: 5 < 10, go left to -4; 5 > -4, go right; right of -4 is empty => 5 is right child of -4.
Insert 3: 3 < 10, go left to -4; 3 > -4, go right to 5; 3 < 5, go left; left of 5 is empty => 3 is left child of 5.
Insert 20: 20 > 10, go right; right of 10 is empty => 20 is right child of 10.
Insert 1: 1 < 10, go left to -4; 1 > -4, go right to 5; 1 < 5, go left to 3; 1 < 3, go left; left of 3 is empty => 1 is left child of 3.
Insert 15: 15 > 10, go right to 20; 15 < 20, go left; left of 20 is empty => 15 is left child of 20.
steps.note: The question mentions node 19 in the prose but the image clearly shows 15. Using 15 as the target node.

steps.title: Trace path from node 15 to root
steps.formula_raw: number of edges = depth of node
steps.apply:
Tree structure:
- Root: 10 (depth 0)
- 10's left: -4 (depth 1), 10's right: 20 (depth 1)
- -4's right: 5 (depth 2), 20's left: 15 (depth 2)
- 5's left: 3 (depth 3)
- 3's left: 1 (depth 4)

Node 15 is at depth 2 (path: 15 -> 20 -> 10).
Number of edges = 2.
steps.note: The path from 15 to root: 15 (left child of 20) -> 20 (right child of 10) -> 10 (root). This is 2 edges.

steps.title: Final count
steps.apply: The path from node 15 to root 10 traverses exactly 2 edges: edge(15,20) and edge(20,10). The number of edges = 2.
steps.note: Note: the prose hint mentions '19' but the image shows the inserted values are 10, -4, 5, 3, 20, 1, 15 and asks about node 15.

solution.result: The number of edges in the path from node 15 to the root node (10) is 2. Answer: 2.

questions.formula_note: BST insertion: compare with current node and go left/right; the depth of a node equals the number of edges from it to the root.
